unit.title: How much, how fast and how far?

title: How far? The extent of chemical change
summary: Many reactions do not run to completion — they settle into a dynamic equilibrium where forward and reverse reactions continue at equal rates and concentrations stay constant. This lesson builds the whole of R2.3: what dynamic equilibrium is and how to recognise it, Le Chatelier's principle and how to predict the effect of changing concentration, pressure/volume and temperature (and why a catalyst never shifts the position), and the equilibrium constant Kc — how to write its expression and read its magnitude as a measure of extent. The HL extension adds the reaction quotient Q versus Kc and Kc calculations. Every worked example is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards the marks.
learningObjectives: Describe dynamic equilibrium in a closed system as the state where the forward and reverse reactions occur at equal rates so that macroscopic concentrations remain constant.
State the characteristics of a system at equilibrium and distinguish equilibrium from a reaction that has simply stopped.
Apply Le Chatelier's principle to predict the effect of changing concentration, pressure/volume and temperature on the position of equilibrium, and explain why a catalyst does not shift it.
Construct the expression for the equilibrium constant Kc and interpret its magnitude to deduce the extent of reaction.
(HL) Deduce the direction of reaction by comparing the reaction quotient Q with Kc, and calculate Kc from equilibrium concentrations.
Present reasoning so that method marks (M) and answer/conclusion marks (A), with error-carried-forward (ECF), protect the score.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL; the reaction quotient and Kc calculations are HL.

simpleExplanation.title: A Balanced Tug-of-War
simpleExplanation.summary: In a closed container, a reversible reaction never truly stops. The forward and reverse reactions keep going at exactly the same rate, so the amounts of everything stay constant even though molecules are still reacting. The equilibrium constant, Kc, is a single number that tells you where that balance sits — mostly products, mostly reactants, or a bit of both.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture a tug-of-war where both teams pull with exactly equal force: the rope is still under tension and both teams are still working hard, but the flag in the middle does not move. That is dynamic equilibrium — busy at the molecular level, yet unchanging on the outside. A large Kc means the flag sits deep in the products' territory; a small Kc means it barely left the reactants' side.
steps: Confirm the system is closed and reversible, so nothing escapes and both directions can occur.
Recognise equilibrium: forward rate = reverse rate, so concentrations become constant (not necessarily equal).
To predict a shift, apply Le Chatelier's principle — the system moves to partially oppose whatever change you impose.
To judge the extent, write the Kc expression (products over reactants, each raised to its coefficient) and read its magnitude.

flashcards.back: In a closed system, the state reached when the forward and reverse reactions occur at equal rates, so the concentrations of reactants and products remain constant. The reaction has not stopped — both directions continue.

Reached in a CLOSED system; forward rate = reverse rate; macroscopic properties (concentration, colour, pressure) are constant; it is dynamic, not static; can be approached from either direction and reaches the same position.

At equilibrium the concentrations of reactants and products stop changing, but they are almost never equal to each other. 'Constant concentrations' and 'equal concentrations' are completely different claims.
If a change is imposed on a system at equilibrium, the position of equilibrium shifts in the direction that partially opposes (counteracts) the change, until a new equilibrium is established.
Increasing the concentration of a species shifts equilibrium AWAY from it (to remove some of it); decreasing it shifts equilibrium TOWARDS that species (to replace it). Kc is unchanged.

Increasing pressure (decreasing volume) shifts equilibrium towards the side with FEWER moles of gas; decreasing pressure shifts towards MORE moles of gas. If both sides have equal moles of gas, pressure has no effect. Kc is unchanged.
Increasing temperature shifts equilibrium in the ENDOTHERMIC direction; decreasing temperature shifts it in the EXOTHERMIC direction. Temperature is the only change that also alters the value of Kc.
A catalyst speeds up the forward and reverse reactions EQUALLY, so equilibrium is reached faster but the position of equilibrium and the value of Kc are unchanged. Yield is not increased.
For a homogeneous reaction the ratio of product concentrations to reactant concentrations at equilibrium, each raised to its stoichiometric coefficient. Its magnitude measures the extent of reaction.
$K_c = \dfrac{[C]^c[D]^d}{[A]^a[B]^b}$. Concentrations are equilibrium values in mol dm⁻³. Pure solids and pure liquids are omitted; their 'concentration' is constant and absorbed into $K_c$.
$K_c \gg 1$: products favoured, equilibrium lies right, high extent. $K_c \ll 1$: reactants favoured, equilibrium lies left, low extent. $K_c \approx 1$: appreciable amounts of both.
ONLY temperature. Changing concentration, pressure or volume, or adding a catalyst shifts the position of equilibrium but leaves $K_c$ unchanged — the system re-adjusts to restore the same ratio.
Same expression as $K_c$ but evaluated with the concentrations present at ANY moment, not just at equilibrium. Comparing $Q$ with $K_c$ tells you which way the reaction must go.
$Q < K_c$: too few products, reaction goes FORWARD (right). $Q > K_c$: too many products, reaction goes REVERSE (left). $Q = K_c$: system is already at equilibrium.

quickCheck.prompt: A reaction reaches equilibrium in a closed flask. Which statement is correct?
options: The forward and reverse reactions have both stopped
The concentrations of reactants and products are equal
The forward and reverse reactions occur at equal rates and concentrations are constant
The reaction is now static and no molecules react
quickCheck.answer: The forward and reverse reactions occur at equal rates and concentrations are constant

quickCheck.prompt: A catalyst is added to a gaseous system at equilibrium. What happens to the position of equilibrium and to Kc?
options: The position shifts right and Kc increases
Neither the position nor Kc changes; equilibrium is just reached faster
The position shifts towards fewer moles of gas
Kc increases but the position is unchanged
quickCheck.answer: Neither the position nor Kc changes; equilibrium is just reached faster

quickCheck.prompt: For an exothermic reaction at equilibrium, the temperature is increased. The position of equilibrium and Kc will:
options: Shift right; Kc increases
Shift left (endothermic direction); Kc decreases
Shift left; Kc stays the same
Not shift because temperature does not affect equilibrium

quickCheck.prompt: For N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g), the pressure is increased by reducing the volume. The equilibrium shifts:
options: Left, towards the 4 moles of gas
Right, towards the 2 moles of gas (fewer moles)
It does not shift because pressure has no effect
Right, and this increases the value of Kc
quickCheck.answer: Right, towards the 2 moles of gas (fewer moles)

quickCheck.prompt: (HL) At a given instant the reaction quotient Q is calculated and found to be smaller than Kc. The reaction will:
options: Proceed forward (to the right) to make more product
Proceed in reverse (to the left) to make more reactant
Already be at equilibrium
Stop, because Q must equal Kc to react
quickCheck.answer: Proceed forward (to the right) to make more product

faq.q: Why is equilibrium described as 'dynamic' rather than the reaction simply stopping?
faq.a: Because both reactions are still happening. At equilibrium the forward reaction and the reverse reaction are proceeding at exactly the same rate, so for every product molecule formed, one is converted back. Nothing changes on the outside — colour, concentration and pressure are constant — but at the molecular level the system is constantly reacting in both directions. A reaction that had truly stopped would be static, not dynamic.

faq.q: What is the difference between constant concentrations and equal concentrations?
faq.a: Constant means unchanging over time — that is what equilibrium guarantees. Equal means reactants and products happen to have the same numerical concentration — that is almost never true. A system can sit at equilibrium with, say, 0.90 mol dm⁻³ of product and 0.20 mol dm⁻³ of reactant: those concentrations are constant but very much not equal. Saying 'at equilibrium the concentrations are equal' is one of the most heavily penalised errors in the topic.

faq.q: Why does a catalyst not change the position of equilibrium or the yield?
faq.a: A catalyst lowers the activation energy for the forward and reverse reactions by the same amount, so it speeds both up equally. Because both rates increase in the same proportion, the system reaches the same balance point — just sooner. It changes how fast equilibrium is reached, never where the equilibrium lies, so Kc and the yield are unchanged.

faq.q: What is the difference between the reaction quotient Q and the equilibrium constant Kc? (HL)
faq.a: They use the identical algebraic expression, but Q is evaluated with whatever concentrations are present at a given moment, whereas Kc uses the concentrations at equilibrium. Comparing them predicts the direction of change: if Q < Kc the system has too little product and shifts forward; if Q > Kc it has too much product and shifts back; if Q = Kc it is already at equilibrium. Kc is a fixed value at a given temperature; Q changes as the reaction proceeds until it equals Kc.

faq.q: Why are pure solids and liquids left out of the Kc expression?
faq.a: The effective concentration of a pure solid or pure liquid is its density, which does not change as the reaction proceeds. Because that value is constant, it is absorbed into the constant Kc rather than written explicitly. Only species whose concentrations genuinely change during the reaction — gases and aqueous solutes — appear in the expression.

sections.content: We often picture a reaction running left to right until the reactants are used up. Many of the most important reactions in chemistry do not behave that way: in a closed container they are reversible and settle into a state called dynamic equilibrium, where the forward and reverse reactions run at equal rates and the amounts of everything stop changing. This lesson answers two questions about such systems — which way will the equilibrium move if we disturb it (Le Chatelier's principle), and how far did the reaction get in the first place (the equilibrium constant, Kc). As always, the worked examples are marked exactly the way our engine marks your own practice.
Dynamic equilibrium and its characteristics

When a reversible reaction takes place in a closed system — one that cannot exchange matter with its surroundings — the forward reaction is fast at first and the reverse reaction is slow, because there is little product to react back. As product builds up, the reverse rate rises and the forward rate falls. Eventually the two rates become equal. From that moment the concentrations of every species stay constant, even though molecules are still reacting in both directions at the same pace. This is dynamic equilibrium: dynamic because reactions continue, equilibrium because nothing changes on the outside.

items: Equilibrium is reached only in a **closed system** (no matter enters or leaves).
At equilibrium the **forward rate equals the reverse rate**.
**Macroscopic properties are constant** — concentration, colour and (for gases) pressure no longer change.
Equilibrium is **dynamic, not static**: both reactions are still occurring.
Constant does **not** mean equal — reactant and product concentrations are usually different from each other.
The same equilibrium position is reached whether you start from reactants or from products.

sections.content: Le Chatelier's principle

Le Chatelier's principle lets us predict what happens when a system at equilibrium is disturbed: the position of equilibrium shifts in the direction that partially opposes the change imposed, until a new equilibrium is established. The word 'partially' matters — the system reduces the effect of the change but never fully cancels it. Three disturbances are examinable: changing a concentration, changing pressure or volume, and changing temperature.

items: **Concentration:** increasing the amount of a species shifts equilibrium AWAY from it; decreasing a species shifts equilibrium TOWARDS it. (Removing a product, for example, pulls the reaction forward.)

**Pressure / volume (gases only):** increasing pressure — usually by decreasing the volume — shifts equilibrium towards the side with FEWER moles of gas; decreasing pressure shifts towards MORE moles of gas. If both sides have the same number of moles of gas, changing pressure has no effect on the position.
**Temperature:** raising the temperature shifts equilibrium in the ENDOTHERMIC direction; lowering it shifts equilibrium in the EXOTHERMIC direction. You must know the sign of ΔH to decide which way.
**Catalyst:** speeds up forward and reverse reactions EQUALLY, so equilibrium is reached faster but its position — and the yield — is unchanged.

sections.content: Only one of these disturbances changes the value of the equilibrium constant. Changing concentration or pressure shifts the position but the system re-adjusts to restore the same Kc; a catalyst leaves both position and Kc untouched. Temperature is the exception: it changes the position AND the value of Kc, because it changes the balance of the forward and reverse rates themselves.

sections.question: Ethanoic acid and ethanol form an ester in a reversible reaction: CH₃COOH(l) + C₂H₅OH(l) ⇌ CH₃COOC₂H₅(l) + H₂O(l). The forward reaction is used to make the ester. State and explain, using Le Chatelier's principle, TWO changes that would increase the equilibrium yield of the ester. [4]
sections.solution:
**Change 1 — remove a product (or add a reactant).**
Continuously removing the ester (or the water) as it forms, OR adding an excess of one reactant such as ethanol, disturbs the balance.  [M1: correct change stated]
By Le Chatelier's principle the equilibrium shifts to oppose the change: removing a product shifts equilibrium to the RIGHT to replace it (adding a reactant shifts it right to use the extra reactant up), increasing the yield of ester.  [A1: correct direction linked to increased yield]

**Change 2 — use a suitable concentration of reactant / drive with excess.**
Increasing the concentration of a reactant shifts equilibrium AWAY from that reactant, i.e. to the right, forming more product.  [M1: correct change stated]
Because the position moves towards the products, the equilibrium yield of the ester rises.  [A1: correct direction linked to increased yield]

*Note what does NOT help: adding a catalyst reaches equilibrium faster but does not change the yield, and pressure has no useful effect because all species are liquids, not gases.*

sections.content: The equilibrium constant, $K_c$

Le Chatelier tells us which way a system moves; the equilibrium constant tells us how far the reaction got. For a homogeneous reaction at a fixed temperature, once equilibrium is reached the ratio of product concentrations to reactant concentrations takes a constant value called Kc. The 'c' signals that it is written in terms of molar concentrations (mol dm⁻³).
aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD \qquad K_c = \frac{[C]^c[D]^d}{[A]^a[B]^b}

items: Square brackets $[\;]$ mean the equilibrium molar concentration (mol dm⁻³) of a species.
Products go on top (numerator); reactants go on the bottom (denominator).
Each concentration is raised to the power of its stoichiometric coefficient from the balanced equation.
Pure solids and pure liquids are OMITTED — their concentration is constant and is absorbed into $K_c$.
$K_c$ is constant at a given temperature; it changes value only if the temperature changes.

sections.content: The magnitude of Kc is a direct measure of the extent of reaction. A large value means the top of the fraction dominates, so products are favoured; a small value means reactants dominate.

items: $K_c \gg 1$ (roughly $> 10^3$): equilibrium lies far to the RIGHT; the mixture is mostly products; the reaction goes nearly to completion.
$K_c \ll 1$ (roughly $< 10^{-3}$): equilibrium lies far to the LEFT; the mixture is mostly reactants; the reaction barely proceeds.
$K_c \approx 1$: appreciable amounts of both reactants and products are present at equilibrium.

sections.question: Hydrogen and iodine reach equilibrium: H₂(g) + I₂(g) ⇌ 2HI(g). (a) Write the expression for Kc. (b) At a certain temperature the equilibrium concentrations are [H₂] = 0.20 mol dm⁻³, [I₂] = 0.20 mol dm⁻³ and [HI] = 1.60 mol dm⁻³. Calculate Kc and comment on the extent of reaction. [4]
sections.solution:
**(a) Kc expression.**
$K_c = \dfrac{[HI]^2}{[H_2][I_2]}$ — product on top, squared because its coefficient is 2; both reactants on the bottom.  [M1: correct expression]

**(b) Substitute the equilibrium concentrations.**
$K_c = \dfrac{(1.60)^2}{(0.20)(0.20)} = \dfrac{2.56}{0.040}$  [M1: correct substitution]
$K_c = 64$  (no units, by IB convention)  [A1: correct value]

**Comment on extent.** $K_c = 64$ is well above 1, so the equilibrium lies to the right and products (HI) are favoured — a fairly high extent of reaction, with more product than reactant present at equilibrium.  [A1: valid interpretation of magnitude]

*Because HI appears squared, forgetting the power gives $12.8$ instead of $64$ — a classic lost mark.*

sections.content: Always write the Kc expression before you touch the numbers. Even if the arithmetic later slips, the correct expression usually secures a mark on its own, and it is where error-carried-forward begins. Then check the equation for any (s) or (l) species — leaving them out of the expression is a deliberately common trap.
The reaction quotient, $Q$, versus $K_c$ (HL)

At Higher Level we often want to know the direction a reaction will move before it reaches equilibrium. The reaction quotient Q uses the same expression as Kc, but with the concentrations present at the current instant rather than at equilibrium. Comparing Q with Kc predicts what happens next: the system always moves so that Q heads towards Kc.

items: $Q < K_c$: too little product relative to equilibrium, so the reaction proceeds FORWARD (to the right) to make more product.
$Q > K_c$: too much product relative to equilibrium, so the reaction proceeds in REVERSE (to the left).
$Q = K_c$: the system is already at equilibrium and there is no net change.
Q changes continuously as the reaction proceeds; it stops changing once it equals Kc.

sections.content: At HL you also calculate Kc from equilibrium concentrations, and sometimes work backwards to find a missing concentration by rearranging the expression. The method is identical to the worked example above: write the expression, substitute the equilibrium values, and evaluate — remembering to raise each concentration to its coefficient.
Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Saying 'at equilibrium the concentrations are equal'** — they are CONSTANT, not equal. This confusion is heavily penalised.
**Claiming a catalyst increases yield or shifts the equilibrium** — it only speeds up reaching equilibrium; position, yield and Kc are unchanged.
**Getting the temperature direction wrong** — increasing temperature favours the ENDOTHERMIC direction; you must use the sign of ΔH to decide, not guess.
**Thinking pressure or concentration changes the value of Kc** — they change the POSITION of equilibrium; only a change in temperature changes Kc.
**Shifting the wrong way for pressure** — higher pressure favours FEWER moles of gas; and if both sides have equal moles of gas, pressure has no effect at all.
**Including solids or liquids in the Kc expression** — omit pure (s) and (l); only gases and aqueous species appear.
**Forgetting the stoichiometric powers** — each concentration is raised to its coefficient (e.g. $[HI]^2$, $[H_2]^3$); missing a power gives the wrong Kc.
**(HL) Reversing the Q vs Kc logic** — $Q < K_c$ means go FORWARD; $Q > K_c$ means go reverse.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

This is the showcase for an explanation-style question. In Paper 2 the marks are analytic: each distinct valid point scores 1 mark, typically a reasoning step (M) followed by the conclusion it supports (A). The engine credits each correct point independently and accepts equivalent wording, and error-carried-forward means a sound consequence drawn from an earlier (even mistaken) statement can still score. Study how each of the four marks below is tied to one specific idea.

sections.question: For the Haber process, N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g), ΔH < 0. Explain the effect of (i) increasing the pressure and (ii) increasing the temperature on the position of equilibrium and on the yield of ammonia. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**

**(i) Increasing the pressure.**
The left side has 4 moles of gas (1 N₂ + 3 H₂) and the right side has 2 moles of gas (2 NH₃). By Le Chatelier's principle, increasing the pressure shifts the equilibrium towards the side with FEWER moles of gas to oppose the rise in pressure — that is, to the RIGHT.  [reasoning]
Therefore the equilibrium position moves towards the products and the yield of ammonia INCREASES.  [conclusion]

**(ii) Increasing the temperature.**
The forward reaction is exothermic (ΔH < 0), so the reverse reaction is endothermic. By Le Chatelier's principle, increasing the temperature shifts the equilibrium in the ENDOTHERMIC direction to absorb the added heat — that is, to the LEFT.  [reasoning]
Therefore the equilibrium position moves towards the reactants and the yield of ammonia DECREASES (and Kc decreases too).  [conclusion]

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks (1 mark per distinct valid point):**

- **M mark, part (i) — reasoning.** Awarded for identifying that the product side has fewer moles of gas (2 vs 4) AND stating that higher pressure shifts equilibrium towards fewer moles of gas / to the right. This is the reasoning point.
- **A mark, part (i) — conclusion.** Awarded for the correct consequence: the yield of ammonia increases. It must follow the shift you argued — this is where ECF lives: a student whose reasoning pointed the shift the other way but who then draws the matching yield conclusion is credited for the consistent conclusion.
- **M mark, part (ii) — reasoning.** Awarded for recognising the forward reaction is exothermic (so the reverse is endothermic) AND stating that increasing temperature shifts equilibrium in the endothermic direction / to the left.
- **A mark, part (ii) — conclusion.** Awarded for the correct consequence: the yield of ammonia decreases (accept also 'Kc decreases').

**Accept equivalents.** 'Fewer gas molecules', 'fewer moles of gas' and 'the side with 2 mol of gas' all score the pressure reasoning. 'Shifts to absorb heat / in the endothermic direction / backward' all score the temperature reasoning. Directions (left/right) may be given by words or arrows.

**Bottom line:** four distinct points, four marks — two reasoning (M) and two conclusion (A). A bare answer such as 'yield goes up then down' with no reasoning scores at most the two conclusion marks, and risks losing those too if the engine cannot see the argument behind them. Show the reasoning and the marks are protected.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer exactly this way — point by point, with each distinct valid idea scoring a mark and ECF protecting a consistent conclusion. So spell out both halves of every point: the reasoning (why the equilibrium shifts) AND the conclusion (what happens to the yield). Writing only the conclusion leaves method marks on the table.
Where this leads

Equilibrium ideas run through the rest of the course. Acid–base chemistry is equilibrium with Ka, Kb and Kw; solubility uses the same reasoning; industrial processes such as the Haber and Contact processes are Le Chatelier trade-offs between yield and rate. Fix the two questions of this topic — which way will it move (Le Chatelier) and how far did it get (Kc) — and every later equilibrium becomes a variation on a method you already own.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 answer marked: apply Le Chatelier's principle and reason with Kc, with full working
